content: Part 0: The Deep Breath — Why It's Not Just a Figure of Speech 👦 Nephew: Uncle! Production is broken. Users are seeing the wrong order totals. I've been randomly changing code for twenty minutes and it's getting worse. 👨‍🦳 Uncle: Stop. Close the editor. Take an actual breath. 👦 Nephew: Uncle, this is not the time for meditation— 👨‍🦳 Uncle: It's exactly the time. Here's what's happening in your brain right now, biologically: panic narrows your attention. You start pattern-matching to the last bug you fixed, not the actual bug in front of you, and you start making changes hoping something sticks — what we call "shotgun debugging." It feels like progress because you're typing fast. It is not progress. It's how a 10-minute bug becomes a 3-hour bug, because now you've also introduced two new bugs from your random changes, and you don't even remember what you changed. The deep breath isn't about calm for calm's sake. It's the physical action that switches you from reacting to observing. Everything we're about to do requires observation first. So — breathe, and answer me one question before you touch a single line of code: what, exactly, did you observe, versus what are you assuming? 👦 Nephew: I... assumed the total calculation function was wrong. 👨‍🦳 Uncle: You assumed. You didn't observe. That's assumption number one, and it's probably wrong, because if the calculation function itself was broken, it'd be broken for everyone, always, not intermittently. Let's actually look. Part 1: Reproduce It — In React Terms, Not Vague Terms 👨‍🦳 Uncle: From the debugging handbook, step one is always "reproduce." In React, "reproduce" has a very specific, narrower meaning than in general backend debugging — you need to reproduce it down to which component, in which state, given which props, rendered how many times. The questions I want answered before we open DevTools: • Does it happen on first load, or only after some interaction? • Does it happen every time you do that interaction, or only sometimes? • Does it happen for a specific order, or all orders? • Does refreshing the page fix it, or does it come back? 👦 Nephew: It happens... after I edit an order and come back to the list. Not on first load. Every time. 👨‍🦳 Uncle: Now we have something real. "After editing and coming back" — that's not a calculation bug, that's a data flow bug. Something about the update isn't correctly reaching the list. Already, without opening a single tool, we've eliminated half your assumptions. This is the value of a precise reproduction — it does half the diagnosis for you before you've written a single console.log. The golden rule of React reproduction: always try to shrink it to the smallest possible trigger. "The whole checkout is broken" is not a reproduction. "Clicking 'Mark Paid' on any order in the list shows the old total for exactly that one order until I refresh" is a reproduction you can actually act on. Part 2: Open React DevTools — Your X-Ray Machine 👦 Nephew: Okay, now can I open the browser console and start console.log-ing everything? 👨‍🦳 Uncle: That's like a doctor reaching for a scalpel before taking an X-ray. Install React DevTools first (the browser extension) — it gives you two tabs, Components and Profiler, and both will tell you more in thirty seconds than an hour of scattered console.logs. The Components tab 👨‍🦳 Uncle: Click on your OrderRow component in the tree. On the right, you'll see its current props and state, live, updating in real time as you interact with the app. ▼ OrdersPage ▼ OrderRow (props: { order: {...}, onMarkPaid: fn }) order: { id: "123", amount: 450, status: "paid" } Enter fullscreen mode Exit fullscreen mode 👦 Nephew: So I can just... look at what props the component actually received, instead of guessing? 👨‍🦳 Uncle: Exactly. Click "Mark Paid," then look again — did order.amount or order.status actually update in the props panel? If the props panel shows the correct new data but the screen still shows the old value, your bug is inside the component's rendering logic. If the props panel itself still shows old data, your bug is upstream — in your data layer, your cache, or your parent component. 👦 Nephew: (clicks around) The props panel shows the old amount even after I edit it! 👨‍🦳 Uncle: There it is — we just eliminated OrderRow as a suspect entirely, in under a minute, without writing a single log statement. The bug is upstream. This is what "verify each layer separately," from the original handbook, looks like in React specifically — DevTools lets you check each layer of the component tree like checking each floor of a building for a leak, without tearing open every wall. The "highlight updates when components render" setting 👨‍🦳 Uncle: There's a settings toggle in React DevTools — "Highlight updates when components render." Turn it on, and every component that re-renders flashes a colored border on screen, live. This single feature answers a question that's impossible to answer just by reading code: is this component even re-rendering when I expect it to? 👦 Nephew: (clicks Mark Paid) ...OrderRow isn't flashing at all when I click it! 👨‍🦳 Uncle: Now we know two things for certain: the data isn't updating, AND the component isn't even attempting to re-render — which tells us this isn't a "stale render" problem, it's that the state update itself never reached this component. We haven't found the root cause yet, but we've narrowed the entire investigation from "something's wrong with orders" to "something's wrong between the mutation firing and the store notifying this specific component." That's the power of observing before guessing. Part 3: The Profiler — Understanding Why Something Rendered (or Didn't) 👦 Nephew: DevTools has a Profiler tab too, right? What's that for versus the Components tab? 👨‍🦳 Uncle: Components tab answers "what does this look like right now." Profiler answers "what happened over time, and why." Click record, perform your action (edit the order), stop recording, and you'll see a flame graph — remember this concept from our CPU debugging conversation, same idea, but for React renders specifically. Render #1 (initial load) ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ │ OrdersPage │ │ ├─ OrderRow (order 1) │ │ ├─ OrderRow (order 2) │ │ └─ OrderRow (order 3) │ └──────────────────────────────────┘ Render #2 (after clicking "Mark Paid" on order 2) ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ │ OrdersPage ← re-rendered │ │ ├─ OrderRow (order 1) ← skipped │ <- memo working correctly │ ├─ OrderRow (order 2) ← skipped │ <- SUSPICIOUS — this one should update! │ └─ OrderRow (order 3) ← skipped │ └──────────────────────────────────┘ Enter fullscreen mode Exit fullscreen mode 👨‍🦳 Uncle: Click on the suspicious component in the flame graph, and DevTools will actually tell you why it did or didn't render — "props did not change," or "hook changed," or "parent re-rendered." This one feature turns "why isn't this updating" from a guessing game into a direct answer from the tool itself. 👦 Nephew: It says "props did not change" for OrderRow (order 2). So React itself is telling us the new order data never actually reached this component as a prop? 👨‍🦳 Uncle: Exactly — and now our suspect list has shrunk to one very specific place: whatever is responsible for handing order 2's updated data down to this component. In our architecture from Part 1, that's the RTK Query cache. Time to look there. Part 4: The Usual Suspects — React-Specific Bug Categories 👨‍🦳 Uncle: Before we dig into your specific bug, let me give you the mental map of React bugs. Almost every "weird React bug" falls into one of these categories. Once you recognize the shape of a bug, you know exactly where to look. Category 1: The Stale Closure function OrderTimer({ orderId }) { const [seconds, setSeconds] = useState(0); useEffect(() => { const interval = setInterval(() => { setSeconds(seconds + 1); // BUG: "seconds" here is frozen at 0 forever }, 1000); return () => clearInterval(interval); }, []); // empty deps — this effect runs ONCE, closure captures seconds=0 forever return {seconds}s; } Enter fullscreen mode Exit fullscreen mode 👨‍🦳 Uncle: This is the single most common "why isn't my state updating correctly" bug in all of React. The setInterval callback was created once, when seconds was 0, and JavaScript closures capture the variable's value at creation time — so every tick, it computes 0 + 1, forever, never actually incrementing past 1. The fix: use the functional update form, which always receives the truly current value regardless of the closure's age: setSeconds(prev => prev + 1); // always gets the LATEST value, not the closure's frozen one Enter fullscreen mode Exit fullscreen mode 👦 Nephew: So the "shape" of this bug is — state seems frozen or uses an old value, inside a callback or effect? 👨‍🦳 Uncle: That's the fingerprint. Whenever you see "this value seems stuck at an old value inside a timer, event listener, or async callback," suspect a stale closure first. Category 2: The Key Prop Identity Crisis {orders.map((order, index) => ( // BUG: using array INDEX as key ))} Enter fullscreen mode Exit fullscreen mode 👨‍🦳 Uncle: This one causes some of the strangest-looking bugs in React — a form input's typed text appearing in the wrong row after the list is sorted or filtered, or a component's internal state bleeding into the wrong item entirely. Why? React uses key to decide whether a component in a list is "the same one from last render" or "a completely new one." Using array index as key means if the order of the array changes but the index doesn't, React thinks the component at position 2 is still "the same" — even though it now represents a totally different order. {orders.map((order) => ( // FIX: stable, unique identity ))} Enter fullscreen mode Exit fullscreen mode 👦 Nephew: So the fingerprint here is — "state or input from one row appears on a different row after sorting, filtering, or deleting an item"? 👨‍🦳 Uncle: Exactly that fingerprint. The moment you see state "teleporting" to the wrong list item, check the key prop before anything else. Category 3: The useEffect Dependency Trap useEffect(() => { fetchOrderDetails(orderId).then(setDetails); }, []); // BUG: missing orderId in deps — never refetches when orderId changes Enter fullscreen mode Exit fullscreen mode 👨‍🦳 Uncle: This effect runs once, on mount, and never again — so if the component is reused for a different orderId (common in a modal that gets reused for different orders instead of being fully remounted), it keeps showing the first order's details forever. The ESLint plugin eslint-plugin-react-hooks will actually warn you about this — turn it on, and treat every warning as a real bug report, not a suggestion. useEffect(() => { fetchOrderDetails(orderId).then(setDetails); }, [orderId]); // FIX: refetch whenever orderId actually changes Enter fullscreen mode Exit fullscreen mode 👦 Nephew: And the opposite mistake — too many dependencies? 👨‍🦳 Uncle: Just as common — an effect that re-runs on every render because you included a dependency (usually an object or function) that gets recreated fresh every render, even though its actual content never changes. That's exactly where the useCallback/useMemo stability we discussed in Part 1 becomes not just a performance nicety but an actual correctness fix. Category 4: The Race Condition in Async Effects useEffect(() => { fetchOrderDetails(orderId).then(setDetails); // BUG: no protection against out-of-order responses }, [orderId]); Enter fullscreen mode Exit fullscreen mode 👨‍🦳 Uncle: Imagine the user rapidly clicks between Order 1, then Order 2. Two fetches fire. If Order 1's fetch happens to resolve after Order 2's (slower network for whatever reason), you end u
